steps.action: Dabke (Arabic: دبكة also spelled dabka, dabki, d with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, grounded.
steps.text: Dabke (Arabic: دبكة also spelled dabka, dabki, d with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, grounded.

steps.action: Dabke combines circle dance and line dancing and
steps.text: Dabke combines circle dance and line dancing and is widely performed at weddings and other joyous occasions.

steps.action: The line forms from right to left and the leader
steps.text: The line forms from right to left and the leader of the dabke heads the line, alternating between facing the audience and the other dancers.

steps.action: Another theory is that the stomping part of dabk
steps.text: Another theory is that the stomping part of dabke started out as a way of solidifying roofs made of mud.

steps.action: People would stomp on the rooftops of houses tog
steps.text: People would stomp on the rooftops of houses together to help compress the material to compact it to prevent cracks from forming.

steps.action: This eventually evolved into the dancing form of
steps.text: This eventually evolved into the dancing form of dabke that is known today.

steps.action: Typically, the dabke begins with a musician play
steps.text: Typically, the dabke begins with a musician playing a solo on the mijwiz or yarghoul of a Dal Ouna piece, often with two singers accompanying his music.

steps.action: The dancers develop a synchronized movement and
steps.text: The dancers develop a synchronized movement and step and when the singers finish their song, the lawweeh breaks from the semicircle to dance on his own.

steps.action: When the leader of the dabke sees that the men's
steps.text: When the leader of the dabke sees that the men's steps are one, in sync, he instructs the dancers to slow down and begin a movement crossing their right foot in front of the opposite one (their left foot).

steps.action: The lawweeh continues to inform the dancers of t
steps.text: The lawweeh continues to inform the dancers of their basic rhythms, and at this point other guests at the wedding or event occurring will join in the dabke line.

steps.action: Al-Sha’rawiyya (الشعراوية): is limited to men an
steps.text: Al-Sha’rawiyya (الشعراوية): is limited to men and is characterized by strong steps or stomps.

steps.action: It is one of the simplest types of dabke and ver
steps.text: It is one of the simplest types of dabke and very close to the normal one.

steps.action: The lawweeh is the most important element in thi
steps.text: The lawweeh is the most important element in this type of dabke.

steps.action: Al-Karaadiyya (الكرادية) also known as Al-Ṭaiyya
steps.text: Al-Karaadiyya (الكرادية) also known as Al-Ṭaiyyara (الطيارة): is characterized by a lack of a lawweeh and slow movement with an azif (عازف) (flute player) in the middle of the circle.

steps.action: In Palestine, it is characterized by its fast rh
steps.text: In Palestine, it is characterized by its fast rhythm in an open circle, and it is performed from left to right.

steps.action: Al-Farah (الفره): is one of the most active type
steps.text: Al-Farah (الفره): is one of the most active types of dabke and therefore requires a high degree of physical fitness.

steps.action: Al-Ghazal (الغزل): is characterized by three str
steps.text: Al-Ghazal (الغزل): is characterized by three strong stomps of the right foot, and is usually tiring for those dancing.

steps.action: For example, even though one might have heard Al
steps.text: For example, even though one might have heard Ala Dal Ouna sung previously telling a different story in this famous love song, people will still call another song ascribing to the same rhythm and theme as Dal Ouna.

steps.action: The majority of dabke music, being folk songs, i
steps.text: The majority of dabke music, being folk songs, is on maqam Bayati musical mode.

steps.action: Usually made of earthenware or metal, it is plac
steps.text: Usually made of earthenware or metal, it is placed either under the left arm or between the legs and struck in the middle for the strong beats and on the edge for the sharp in-between beats.

steps.action: The sounds of this percussion instrument set the
steps.text: The sounds of this percussion instrument set the rhythm of much Arab music, particularly in the performances of classical pieces.

steps.action: The arghul (يرغول), also known as the yarghoul,
steps.text: The arghul (يرغول), also known as the yarghoul, is commonly used in solos, often accompanied by singers, that begin dabke performances.

steps.action: The Shubabeh (شبابة) is a woodwind instrument tr
steps.text: The Shubabeh (شبابة) is a woodwind instrument traditionally made from reed cane.

steps.action: The Shubabeh is traditionally played by herders
steps.text: The Shubabeh is traditionally played by herders in the wilderness.
